text: As someone based in the UK, I completely understand the frustration. For years, market access to Bitcoin exposure for both institutions and retail investors has been limited for several reasons. Many UK investors have struggled to participate fully in Bitcoin’s performance until recently (eg can now buy via revolut but not everyone wants to do this for tax reasons). Yes, MicroStrategy (MSTR) is a legitimate option, and after the recent pullback (during which they’ve continued to accumulate Bitcoin) the risk/reward does look more compelling. But there are two important things to consider. 1. Fees A lot of UK retail platforms charge a large fee for buying US stocks due to the FX. If it’s a buy/hold then it’s easier to stomach but if you plan to trade it, it will become expensive. 2. Risk / reward profile MSTR for Bitcoin exposure in your ISA makes sense. It’s the most established Bitcoin treasury company global. From a risk perspective, it’s further along the adoption curve: more mature, more institutionalised, and generally more efficiently priced by the market. That said, there is also a UK-listed alternative in The Smarter Web Company (SWC) which is traded on the LSE and been mentioned by a few people here. It’s the largest bitcoin treasury in the UK (30th globally) and while smaller and earlier in its treasury journey, SWC sits at a different point on the adoption curve - particularly within the UK market, where many investors face structural constraints around holding Bitcoin directly. As a UK-listed equity, it fits naturally within an ISA and domestic portfolios, and because it is earlier-stage, it carries a different risk/reward profile: higher volatility/more beta, there’s naturally more execution risk to implementing the playbook (although management have done very well since IPO) but potentially greater upside if the UK market increasingly adopts Bitcoin treasury strategies as an equity story. In simple terms, MSTR represents the established US model; SWC represents earlier-stage UK adoption, same underlying asset exposure, but at different stages of maturity and market development. Can always have a split of both of them in the portfolio which I (and others do). As always, not financial advice but hope this helps.

text: 10 Key Takeaways from the Call between @ZynxBTC , @asjwebley and myself. (All interpretations are my own, with additional commentary where relevant). 1. First of its kind Bitcoin-backed M&A (outside the ecosystem) Zynx noted that SWC’s acquisition of Squarebird is (to the best of knowledge) the first publicly listed Bitcoin treasury company to complete a Bitcoin-backed acquisition outside the Bitcoin ecosystem, both globally and in the UK. By “Bitcoin-backed,” we mean the equity used to part-fund the deal derives its value from the Bitcoin held on the balance sheet. Importantly, this demonstrates management executing on stated strategy, reinforcing credibility, which underpins mNAV premium potential. 2. Disciplined and low risk The acquisition was completed at ~2.5x–3x EBITDA and represented just over 1% of SWC’s balance sheet - a prudent and measured deployment of capital. It also diversifies revenue into a cash-generative business not directly linked to Bitcoin price or other Bitcoin-native activities. 3. Opens the door to further opportunities Andrew mentioned that the Company has already been approached regarding additional transactions. For UK SMEs, exit options are often limited; a known, listed buyer provides certainty and flexibility. SWC’s position may allow it to negotiate attractive terms. 4. A growing balance sheet expands the opportunity set Squarebird represented ~1% of the balance sheet. As Bitcoin appreciates and the balance sheet grows, the scale and type of opportunities available to SWC could increase - management will always assess against the cost of capital and expected returns. 5. Enhances financial resilience Building an operating platform capable of covering Group overheads strengthens the model materially. It reduces a common bear case: “What happens in tougher conditions?” 6. AI is complementary, not disruptive Web design agencies operate more like creative consultants. While AI lowers technical barriers, many businesses lack the time, creative direction or expertise to execute effectively. Clients are willing to pay for strategy, implementation and ongoing support. Human creativity remains a differentiator. 7. Clear integration responsibility Andrew will not oversee day-to-day integration. Mario, who leads acquisition strategy, will manage integration allowing Andrew to remain focused on broader strategic execution as CEO. 8. Coinbase facility increases flexibility The $30m facility enhances operational and financial flexibility, particularly when mNAV trades near 1x. It is not intended as long-term leverage to buy Bitcoin as there are numerous opportunity costs to this. Structural debt to acquire BTC would typically require fixed terms and clear repayment visibility, ideally supported by accretive equity issuance in stronger market conditions. 9. Strategy World constructive, sentiment still fragile Conversations were productive and engagement strong. While Andrew believes much negativity may be priced in, it is premature to call an inflection point. Bitcoin remains influenced by broader equity and macro dynamics. 10. The UK presents a clear structural opportunity. Many institutions are restricted to listed equities within the UK only and/or cannot hold Bitcoin directly, while retail investors may avoid self-custody, lack access to ETNs, or prefer exposure within ISAs and SIPPs. SWC bridges that gap. Subject to confirmation on 3 March, expected inclusion in the FTSE All-Share would also open the door to passive index inflows, further supporting liquidity.

text: Why bitcoin treasury companies can trade at a premium to mNAV? Had some good feedback post the spaces with @ZynxBTC so wanted to put it into writing. In my view, $SWC (and Bitcoin treasury companies more broadly) have four clear sources of demand that can support trading at a premium to mNAV. 1) Investors solving for market access There’s still a meaningful pool of capital that can’t hold spot Bitcoin, ETFs/ETNs. Moreover, many fund managers cannot hold International peers as they have a UK only mandate. 2) Investors seeking outperformance vs Bitcoin Many crypto managers are benchmarked to Bitcoins performance. They’re not just looking for exposure, they want to outperform. Treasury companies tend to have higher volatility for a number of reasons, which is why you’ll often hear them described as offering “amplified Bitcoin.” 3) Investors seeking Bitcoin per share growth The only thing better than Bitcoin is owning more Bitcoin and treasury companies give investors a pathway to long term accumulation. Example: You start with £50k today (Bitcoin at £50k, so 1 BTC of exposure). If in 10 years Bitcoin is £100k, spot gets you £100k and an ETF (assuming 1% annual fees) gets you ~£90k (0.9 BTC). But a Bitcoin treasury company growing Bitcoin per share at ~10% a year would give you ~2.6 BTC of exposure, worth ~£260k. That’s why investors focus on Bitcoin per share growth: they’re not just buying BTC, they’re buying future BTC accretion, which helps explain why treasury companies like can attract demand above mNAV. Investors are willing to pay today for that forward BTC compounding (or the expectation/sustainability of it) and this is why you often hear the phrase "compound Bitcoin". Note the concept of Bitcoin per share (via "accretive dilution") is still very new to a lot of institutional investors and will take time for them to fully understand. 4) Investors/Traders seeking volatility Shorter term participants who are largely mNAV-agnostic. They’re trading volatility, liquidity, and momentum, which supports turnover and price discovery. This group was responsible for a lot of the volume during the 2025 summer peak. Put together, these represent four distinct buyer groups with different motivations and create a structural basis for equity demand above mNAV. Hope this helped with understanding why these companies can trade at a premium to mNAV.

text: Call notes between @Anders_ and @asjwebley * All interpretations are my own. 1. LSE uplisting was a structural change, it unlocks a much bigger investor pool than Aquis. 2. Next near-term catalyst: potential FTSE index inclusion (FTSE All-Share / FTSE Small Cap) → passive/index flows alongside active institutional capital. Confirmation is March 3 with it being made effective on March 20 COB. 3. Liquidity is strategic. Institutions size positions vs ADV (avg daily volume). More liquidity = easier institutional participation + healthier secondary market. 4. Treasury model: works in down/flat/up markets, but best when BTC is rising (ideally “modestly up” vs aggressive spikes). 5. Continued execution despite challenging conditions: SWC announced 10 BTC + 15 BTC purchases recently. Message: they can still accumulate even in weak sentiment. This to me was important as it demonstrates we can still keep the flywheel spinning (even if modest) - every Bitcoin counts. 6. Why BTC yield was ~-0.5% this quarter: one-off LSE listing costs (~£1.5m), higher opex, and tight mNAV making accretive issuance harder. Important to look at BTC yield over longer time periods such as quarters (not days) as instant snap shots may don't necessarily incorporate the full story. 7. Capital strategy: want to raise the “right capital” vs quick-flip capital. Historically all raises have been at-market raises with no discounts. 8. Practical mNAV: issuing equity to buy BTC is tough below ~1.05–1.10 mNAV after fees/slippage. In tight mNAV, execution needs to be precise. 9. Operating business: web services have been relatively flat; goal is to grow operating profit to cover costs so SWC has runway to keep operating during challenging times and is never forced to sell Bitcoin in a bad year (which Andrew has previously said would be an absolute last resort). 10. Focus metrics: (1) BTC per share (2) BTC yield (3) total BTC held (scale). Goal isn’t just BTC/share - it’s also growing total BTC held. Even if BTC/share is flat but you can increase the stack, this adds scale and scale strengthens the whole model (e.g lower cost ratio, better credit perception etc). Upcoming events: Bitcoin Treasuries Unconference UK (May 29, Bristol) where Phong Le announced y'day that he will be doing a virtual presentation (big stamp of credibility) and Andrew speaking at Strategy World (Vegas) & other events. Tickets available on the SWC website.

text: Call notes on the excellent interview between @RichOnMacro and @asjwebley and @Croesus_BTC Thanks to @RichOnMacro for being a supporter of SWC since the start and helping us expand awareness via his brand and platform. * All interpretations are my own. Andrew described ringing the LSE opening bell with ~100 shareholders, the team, and family as a surreal, defining moment (joking it was “up there” with his wedding day). They deliberately brought shareholders into the moment via a raffle so it wasn’t just management celebrating - it felt like a shared milestone (personal note: was great to meet so many people and I know how much this gesture was appreciated). He framed the move from Aquis → LSE Main Market as a major step-change: Aquis was the fast/low-cost launchpad, but the Main Market is key for global institutional access and index eligibility (FTSE All-Share / Small Cap first, with ambitions longer-term). Andrew noted that in <10 months they effectively did “two IPOs,” raised significant capital, built out an experienced team, and established the Bitcoin treasury strategy. His general lesson for founders/CEOs: love what you do, keep showing up every day, and stay focused on long-term execution rather than short term price noise. Andrew and Jesse talked openly about the emotional whiplash of the company’s market cap moving from ~£20m → ~£1bn → ~£150m, and how they stay disciplined through it. Andrew’s core point: don’t anchor on fiat share price - track Bitcoin per share and total Bitcoin stack. He said since the July 2025 peak they’ve grown BTC/share ~50% and roughly doubled the overall stack, even as the share price fell. Other key foundations have been put in place to help solidify long term sustainability throughout market cycles. He added that parts of the playbook were effectively constrained during the LSE listing process, which limited what they could do in late 2025. Jesse emphasised Bitcoin’s fixed-supply mechanics (cap + halvings) and argued corporate treasury buying can outpace new supply, even if price still drops when long term holders sell to diversify or follow “cycle” narratives. Their take on the “cycle”: patterns can become self-fulfilling, but they’re not laws of physics - the long-term setup matters more than short term sentiment. Andrew argued the best backdrop for a Bitcoin treasury company is stable to rising BTC (confidence + capital availability), not a collapsing BTC price. Jesse’s framing: “volatility is vitality” - Bitcoin’s biggest outlier days tend to be up days, and treasury companies can amplify that volatility (accepting drawdowns as part of the deal). They outlined what the Main Market listing can unlock: more equity capital, index-related flows, and potentially future instruments (e.g preferred structures used by peers) to reach investors who want yield with less volatility. On capital allocation: they’re currently zero debt, but see prudent leverage as a tool. Andrew said at very low mNAV (e.g., ~0.7x) buybacks can be a “screaming buy,” and noted they now have authority to buy back up to 10% if extreme dislocations happen. They avoided a precise end 2026 BTC price call, but suggested bearish sentiment often clusters near bottoms and long term fundamentals remain intact. Jesse flagged hash rate as a key metric to watch, reinforcing the thesis of Bitcoin as a neutral digital reserve asset (“digital gold”).

text: Great to see Andrew getting coverage in bloomberg as part of a wider article. Andrew Webley, chief executive officer of a DAT called The Smarter Web Company Plc, said he has no plans to sell. The company has mostly used equity to fund its Bitcoin purchases, Webley said, eschewing the complex debt structures used by some other DATs. Maintaining a healthy operating business alongside its Bitcoin treasury is what will keep the firm — whose main business is web design — afloat where others fail, he said. After initially debuting on a smaller UK exchange, The Smarter Web Company moved its listing to London’s main market on Tuesday. “I’d probably sell you one of my arms before I’d sell you any of the Bitcoin,” he said.

text: It’s been a week of two halves. The LSE listing marked a tremendous milestone, yet there’s no denying that price action across asset classes has been painful. In conversations with hedge funds, many noted that yesterday, and this week more broadly, has been among the most difficult in quite some time. This isn’t just about Bitcoin. We’ve seen an extreme unwind across markets, driven by widespread deleveraging and de-risking from funds. That’s why it’s always important to step back and look at the broader landscape, rather than viewing events in isolation. See some colour below: “Was another violent session with all the US majors tumbling (led by Small Caps for a change) after weak labor market data. Market breadth deterioratedwith only 200 names in the SPX up on the day. Nasdaq extended losses below its 100DMA (with the 200DMA/No lows around 24,000 ish the next stop for support). The S&P broke through its 50DMA and tested the 100DMA (6,794). Momentum unwind (winners being sold and losers being bought) showed no signs of recovery from yesterday's meltdown. A larger unwind of winners is the pain trade given where nets are in our PB book (89th percentile on 1yr). High velocity feeling some pain with Bitcoin down hard, Global Rare Earths -560bps, Non Profitable Tech -500bps, and Liquid Most Short -495bps, which are all seeing -1.5sigma moves or greater. Bitcoin traded back below Trump election lows with a $62k handle today (at the same level it was at in Nov 2021). Biggest daily drop in Bitcoin since the FTX collapse, that is a five sigma crash relative to the last three years. Bitcoin is now down 50% from its highs. And this latest purge has pushed Bitcoin to 3rd most oversold ever.”

text: There’s no getting away from how tough sentiment and price action are right now. I’ve been in Bitcoin since 2018 and working in the sector professionally since 2022, and across multiple cycles one pattern tends to repeat: the inflection point is usually preceded by peak FUD. That’s where I believe we are now, or at least very close. I know the losses hurt, and no one can call the exact bottom but this really is the moment to zoom out, filter the noise as much as possible, and refocus on fundamentals which, in my view, remain constructive over the medium to long term for both Bitcoin and Smarter Web.

What I took away is that there is an enormous collective belief in the mission of what SWC are trying to build. Price action in Bitcoin has been tough, there’s no getting away from that. However I hope people can recognise that fundamentally the company is in the strongest position it’s been in since IPO and I remain encouraged. Looking forward to seeing many of you again soon.

text: Ahead of tomorrow’s relisting, it’s hard to ignore the tougher price action in Bitcoin. That said, short term price moves are always the loudest signal, but rarely the most important one. What matters more, in my view, is whether the strategy, balance sheet discipline and operating foundations are robust enough to endure this part of the cycle, which I believe they are. We’re building for durability across cycles, including periods where sentiment is cautious and price action is challenging, like we’re seeing now. It’s also worth remembering that we’re only nine months into the journey. Each phase teaches us more about how to operate within the market conditions we’re given. It’s a dynamic, iterative process that requires patience. As I said yesterday, there’s a reasonable argument that relisting at a time when sector sentiment has reset provides a cleaner baseline from which to measure progress. Expectations are more grounded, narratives less inflated. I remain focused on the long arc rather than the daily chart. This is still the early innings in what we see as a multi year journey, and much of the most important work happens away from the screen. Looking forward to meeting many of you tomorrow.

engagement.note: These figures were correct as of May 14, 2026, 9:00 AM London time

text: Bitcoin’s recent price action and weekend sell off are a reminder of the asset class we operate in - one where periods of absolute (and relative) outperformance are inevitably accompanied by drawdowns. They’re two sides of the same coin: volatility. I’m an optimist and whilst broader sentiment may feel challenging next week (and potentially beyond), there’s a reasonable case to be made that relisting in a more challenging environment provides a clearer and more honest starting point for this next stage of the journey, rather than one shaped by elevated sentiment or unusually favourable conditions. I’ve been involved in the space professionally since 2022. During my time at Galaxy, we spoke with many of the most thoughtful participants in the market and I remain convinced (while acknowledging my own biases) that whilst immediate price action is tough, the long term backdrop remains supportive for both Bitcoin and selective treasury companies. I’m very much looking forward to meeting people on Tuesday. Regardless of what the screen says, my own approach is to stay constructive and positive, remembering that we’re only nine months into a ten year plan and that a great deal of important foundational work has already been put in place.
